title: On the geometric algebras of the Ising model
authors: N. Johnson, D. Marenduzzo, A. Morozov, E. Orlandini, G. M. Vasil
abstract: We revisit the classical transfer matrix solution of the one- and two-dimensional Ising model from the perspective of Clifford and conformal geometric algebras. Building on Kaufman's spinor formulation, we show that all elements entering the solution, including the transfer matrix, its eigenvectors, and the quasiparticle excitations, admit a natural and unified interpretation as elements of an appropriate conformal Clifford algebra. In particular, the transfer matrix can be viewed as a dilation generated by a conformal bivector, while its eigenvectors correspond to null combinations of Clifford generators, closely paralleling the emergence of Majorana fermionic degrees of freedom. In the two-dimensional case, the standard eigenvalue equation for the row-to-row transfer matrix is reinterpreted as a dispersion relation for quasiparticle excitations, exposing the connection between the Ising model and a theory of free Majorana fermions. While all the explicit exact results recovered are well known, this geometric reformulation provides a unified algebraic framework which is compact and physically interpretable. Specifically, this clarifies the role of scale transformations, fermionic modes, and duality in the Ising model. We believe this approach offers a useful pedagogical complement to more conventional fermionic, Grassmann, or field theoretic treatments.
summary: This paper provides a new geometric perspective on the exactly solvable 1D and 2D Ising models. By using Clifford and Conformal Geometric Algebras, the authors reinterpret the transfer matrix as a dilation and the model's excitations as Majorana-like fermions. While the underlying physical results are well-established, this framework offers a more compact and unified algebraic description of scale transformations, duality, and quasiparticle dynamics.
main_problem: The paper seeks to provide a unified, geometrically interpretable algebraic framework for the 1D and 2D Ising models using Clifford and Conformal Geometric Algebras (CGA).
main_result: The authors show that the transfer matrix, eigenvectors, and quasiparticle excitations can be naturally represented as elements of a conformal Clifford algebra, where the transfer matrix acts as a dilation and eigenvectors correspond to Majorana-like modes.
method: The study employs Clifford algebra Cl(1,1) and Cl(n,n) to reformulate the transfer-matrix solution, utilizing Hestenes spinors and the properties of conformal bivectors to reinterpret the model's dynamics.
model_or_system: The paper analyzes the classical 1D and 2D Ising models on square lattices with periodic boundary conditions, focusing on the transfer-matrix formalism and the emergence of domain walls and quasiparticles.
key_observables: Partition function, transfer matrix eigenvalues, dispersion relation, and correlation length.
important_parameters: Coupling constant (K), dual coupling (K*), and the critical temperature/coupling (Kc).
main_assumptions: The 2D model assumes periodic boundary conditions and the absence of an external magnetic field.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a domain wall in the 1D Ising model as a spin-flip via a bivector; Figure 2 displays the dispersion relation across high-temperature, critical, and low-temperature regimes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the Clifford algebra framework for the 1D model, extends the formalism to the 2D case using Cl(n,n), derives the dispersion relation and critical behavior, and concludes with a discussion on the pedagogical value of this geometric approach.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Sketch of a domain wall in the 1D Ising model, which can be associated with an e+−bivector acting on the edge which separates the two domains. This is because e+−=  0 1 1 0

Figure 2. Dispersion relation showing the angle αr = log(λr) (logarithm of the eigenvalues of M and of W) versus r/n (normalised wavenumber). (a) Low K (high temperature) phase at K = 0.4: there is a gap in the system. (b) Critical behaviour at K = Kc ≃0.4407 (defined by K∗ c = Kc): the gap closes, and a zero-energy mode (low-energy quasi-particle excitation) appears around r = n/2. (c) High K (low temperature) phase: the gap has reopened.
